policy_statement: Electrify 90% of total existing City-owned buildings by 2035.
mechanism_description: The city electrifies existing publicly owned buildings, shifting heating and other end uses from fossil fuels to electricity and reducing on-site combustion emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy service (heat/hot water/cooking) → fuel switch to electricity → ↓ on-site fossil combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Retrofit/replace fossil-fueled building systems in public buildings (space heating, water heating, cooking, sometimes backup) with electric alternatives (e.g., air/ground-source heat pumps, electric resistance where applicable, induction cooking).  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced/avoided combustion of natural gas/oil/propane on-site lowers direct CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions; remaining emissions shift upstream to electricity generation.  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in life-cycle GHG emissions (especially where electricity is low/declining-carbon), with the primary and immediate effect being elimination of on-site combustion emissions from buildings.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing building GHG emissions by eliminating on-site fossil fuel combustion through electrification of end uses.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Electrify building end uses → displace on-site fossil combustion → lower GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve energy efficiency and indoor air quality, the defining mechanism is fuel switching that removes direct combustion emissions; efficiency is secondary/optional and not required for the mechanism to operate.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the grid is highly carbon-intensive, near-term net reductions may be smaller or could increase upstream emissions; however the mechanism’s largest climate-relevant lever remains direct fossil displacement and enabling deeper decarbonization as the grid cleans.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that electrifies existing buildings to replace on-site fossil fuel end uses shares the same dominant mitigation pathway of combustion displacement (regardless of whether buildings are public or private).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy should be tagged additionally as “energy efficiency,” “renewable electricity procurement,” or “municipal operations” depends on policy-specific details not assumed here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG benefits depend on grid emissions factors and technology choice (e.g., heat pumps vs resistance), and some policies may pair electrification with efficiency or clean power procurement; those co-benefits/secondary mechanisms should be assessed at the individual policy level.
co_benefits: Potential air quality improvements, reduced indoor combustion pollutants, and possible operational cost savings/energy resilience benefits (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented via a municipal mandate or capital investment program, but the text itself is framed purely as a numeric target with a timeline, so it is classified as target-setting.
